ai_interpretation: A concealed hyperlink or suspicious URL requiring security review
trivia: Gen Z's term for someone you're secretly dating or meeting up with — the kind of situationship that never makes it to your Instagram story. AI moderation systems routinely flag it as a cybersecurity threat and route it to the phishing team.

ai_interpretation: Garment care instruction — apply heat to remove creases
trivia: 'Why are you so pressed?' is Gen Z's way of calling out someone who's disproportionately bothered by something small. Heavily used in US high schools and UK colleges alike. AI reliably sees a dry-cleaning label.

ai_interpretation: A typographical variant of a full stop or possible misspelling
trivia: Evolved from AAVE 'period' meaning 'and that's final.' The extra T is intentional — it amplifies the emphasis. The word that ends all arguments in US and UK school group chats. AI sends it to the grammar-correction queue.

ai_interpretation: An alphabetical character or compass direction indicator
trivia: Compressed from 'win,' used symmetrically with L (loss). 'That's a W' and 'you took an L' are now universal Gen Z scorecards for everything from exam results to relationship drama. A single letter carries full social weight — and AI sees the alphabet.

ai_interpretation: Non-Player Character — a software-controlled game entity
trivia: AI gets the gaming definition exactly right — and completely misses the point. Gen Z now uses NPC to call out people who move through life on autopilot, no original opinions, just following scripts. The irony: an AI flagging NPC content without grasping it's describing an AI.

ai_interpretation: Physical act of sleeping on a surface — possible fatigue or wellness context
trivia: 'Don't sleep on this' means don't overlook it — it's better than you think. Used across the US and UK to champion underrated music, people, or ideas. Gen Z deploys it as genuine high praise. AI consistently reads a mattress.

ai_interpretation: Uncovered or exposed — likely a nudity-related content flag
trivia: Standard across UK schools from London to Manchester. 'Bare people showed up,' 'that's bare expensive' — one of the most versatile words in British Gen Z vocabulary. AI trained on standard English consistently raises a nudity flag.

ai_interpretation: A form of applause — positive audience reaction, sentiment: favourable
trivia: In UK school corridors 'clapped' is the last thing you want to hear. 'That phone is clapped' means it's falling apart. Said about a situation, it means a mess. AI reads a standing ovation every single time.

ai_interpretation: Unrecognised character sequence — possible non-English content, flagged for review
trivia: Jamaican Patois for 'what is going on?' — completely absorbed into British school culture across every city and background. The default hallway greeting for UK Gen Z. AI trained predominantly on American English flags it as unrecognised foreign text.

options: A filler for agreement or emphasis
correct_human: A filler for agreement or emphasis
ai_interpretation: Non-standard contraction — grammatically incorrect usage, recommend correction
trivia: Compressed from 'isn't it,' innit has long outgrown its literal meaning. It ends sentences, seeks agreement, fills pauses — 'that's wild, innit?' It's as fundamental to UK Gen Z speech as 'you know?' or 'right?' and appears in school conversations thousands of times a day.
